content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

February 24, 1994

James W. Brown, Chief
Adoptions Branch
Department of Social Services
744 P Street, M.S. 19-69
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-94-052

Dear Mr. Brown:

This is in response to your letter requesting advice regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")¹ regulating the receipt of gifts and honoraria.

Please note that this letter is based on the facts presented to us. The Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

As the Chief of the Adoptions Branch of the Department of Social Services, may you accept a gift of travel to Russia and Belarus, related lodging and subsistence, from Children of Light, a California nonprofit organization?

CONCLUSION

If Children of Light is a charitable organization exempt from taxation under Internal Revenue Code Section 501(c)(3) and the travel is related to a governmental purpose or an issue of public policy, the gift of actual travel and related lodging and subsistence received from Children of Light would be exempt from the gift limits of the Act. However, the gift of travel and related lodging and subsistence would be disclosable on your annual statement of economic interests.

¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections 18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-94-052
Page 2


     In addition, pursuant to Section 87100 of the Act you would
be required to disqualify yourself from governmental decisions
that will foreseeably and materially affect the donor, Children of
Light, for 12 months after the receipt of the payment.

                              FACTS

     The facts on which this letter is based were obtained in my
telephone conversation of February 7, 1994, with Morgan Bates,
Executive Director of Children of Light and from your letter of
February 15, 1994.

     Children of Light is a nonprofit organization that assists
children in Russia and Eastern Europe by providing humanitarian
aid and arranging adoptions in the United States. In furtherance
of these goals, representatives of Children of Light have met with
officials in Eastern Europe.

     You are the Chief of the Adoptions Branch of the Department
of Social Services. Children of Light has asked you to accompany
them on the next trip to Eastern Europe. They have asked you to
meet with officials in Russia and Belarus to discuss international
adoptions, adoption issues and procedures, and the health and
welfare of the children involved in such adoptions. Children of
Light will use its funds to pay the cost of actual travel and
related lodging and subsistence.

                             ANALYSIS

     As I discussed with Ms. Bates on February 7, 1994, Section
89505(a) now provides, in pertinent part:

          (a)  No member of a state board or commission,
     and no designated employee of a state agency, shall
     accept gifts with a total value of more than two
     hundred fifty dollars ($250) in a calendar year
     from any single source, if the member or employee
     would be required to report the receipt of income
     or gifts from that source on his or her statement
     of economic interest. This paragraph shall not
     apply to any part-time member of the governing
     board of any public institution of higher
     education, unless the member is also an elected
     official.[2]


     2   Pursuant to Section 89504, the Commission raised the gift limit
from $250 to $270. It is scheduled to consider another adjustment
in 1995.

Our File No. A-94-052
Page 3


     You are a designated employee subject to the limit in Section
89505(a).  However, where a designated employee receives a gift of
travel, Section 89506 controls whether the gift is subject to the
gift limit.  (Section 89505(b); Section 89504(b)(1).)  Section
89506 provides:

     (a)  Payments, advances, or reimbursements,
for travel, including actual transportation and
related lodging and subsistence which is reasonably
related to a legislative or governmental purpose,
or to an issue of state, national, or international
public policy, is not prohibited or limited by this
chapter if either of the following apply:

* * *

     (2) The travel is provided by ... a
nonprofit charitable or religious organization
which is exempt from taxation under Section
501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code, or by
a person domiciled outside the United States
which substantially satisfies the requirements
for tax exempt status under Section 501(c)(3)
of the Internal Revenue Code.

                              Section 89506(a), emphasis
                              added.

     You stated that you would attend meetings with government
officials in Russia and Belarus to discuss the provision of
humanitarian aid and adoption of children in those countries.  It
appears from your facts that the purpose of the travel is
reasonably related to a governmental purpose or an issue of state,
national, or international public policy pursuant to the exception
in Section 89506.

     Moreover, according to the information Ms. Bates provided and
the information in your letter, the donor of the gift will be
Children of Light, a nonprofit organization.  If the donor,
Children of Light, is a charitable organization exempt from
taxation under Internal Revenue Code Section 501(c)(3) (or is a
foreign entity meeting the same requirements), the gift of actual
travel and related lodging and subsistence would be exempt from
the limits of the Act.  (See also, Regulation 18950.1(b).)

     However, as I explained in our telephone conversation, the
receipt of a gift of travel and related lodging and subsistence
would be disclosable on your annual statement of economic
interests in accordance with Section 87207(c).  Also, pursuant to

Our File No. A-94-052
Page 4

Section 87100 of the Act, you would be required to disqualify
yourself from governmental decisions that will foreseeably and
materially affect the donor, Children of Light, for 12 months
after the receipt of the payment.3

Other Payments

Please note, however, that any other costs borne by the
Children of Light or other sources would need to be analyzed
separately. You should contact us for further advice should this
be the case.

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.4

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division

_______________________________

3 Both you and Ms. Bates stated that it was unlikely that any
governmental decisions you are involved in will affect Children of
Light.

4 Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.
